---
title: Value of information
---

<!-- colab-badge:start -->

<a href="https://colab.research.google.com/github/pedroliman/heormodel/blob/main/docs/_notebooks/voi.ipynb"><img src="https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg" alt="Open In Colab"></a>

<!-- colab-badge:end -->

This tutorial shows how to run the full value-of-information workflow: expected value of perfect information (EVPI), of partial perfect information (EVPPI), and of sample information (EVSI), continuing from the [full pipeline tutorial](full-pipeline.qmd). The full script is [`examples/voi_tutorial.py`](https://github.com/pedroliman/heormodel/blob/main/examples/voi_tutorial.py).

## Specifying the decision model

We specify two interventions, standard care and a new drug, which differ by an uncertain incremental effect `dq` (quality-adjusted life-years, QALYs) and an uncertain incremental cost `dc`, both Normal.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from heormodel.models import Outcomes
from heormodel.params import Normal, ParameterSet
from heormodel.run import run_psa

WTP = 30_000.0
MU_Q, SD_Q = 0.20, 0.30
MU_C, SD_C = 4_000.0, 8_000.0

# TODO: Here we should just use one of the models like the markov model instead of doing this.
def new_drug(d: pd.DataFrame) -> Outcomes:
    zero = np.zeros(len(d))
    costs = pd.DataFrame({"Standard care": zero, "New drug": d["dc"]}, index=d.index)
    effects = pd.DataFrame({"Standard care": zero, "New drug": d["dq"]}, index=d.index)
    return Outcomes.from_wide(costs, effects)

params = ParameterSet({"dq": Normal(MU_Q, SD_Q), "dc": Normal(MU_C, SD_C)})
draws = params.sample(100_000, seed=2026)
outcomes = run_psa(new_drug, draws, sequential=True).outcomes
```

## Calculating the Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

EVPI is the difference between a decision made with perfect information and one made with current information, an upper bound on the value of any research.

```{python}
from heormodel.voi import evpi

evpi(outcomes, WTP)
```

## Ranking parameters with EVPPI

EVPPI is the value of resolving one parameter while the rest stay uncertain. It identifies which parameters drive the decision uncertainty, so `evppi_ranking` sweeps each and sorts them into a research priority list. The effect `dq` outranks the cost `dc` here because `WTP * SD_Q` exceeds `SD_C`.

```{python}
from heormodel.voi import evppi, evppi_ranking

evppi_ranking(outcomes, draws, WTP)
```

The ranking is threshold-specific: below about 28,000 per QALY the cost ranks higher instead. The default `spline` metamodel suits one or two parameters; `method="gp"` (a Gaussian process) handles groups of many parameters.

## Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI)

EVSI is the value of a study of finite sample size. Consider a two-arm trial measuring the incremental effect, each arm reporting a mean QALY difference with sampling standard deviation `tau = 1 / sqrt(n)`. `simulate_summaries` draws one trial summary per parameter draw from its true `dq`, and `evsi_regression` computes the value from the fitted conditional means.

```{python}
from heormodel.voi import evsi_regression, simulate_summaries

tau = 1.0 / np.sqrt(200)
summaries = simulate_summaries(
    draws, lambda row, r: {"xbar": row["dq"] + r.normal(0.0, tau)}, seed=200)
evsi_regression(outcomes, summaries, WTP)
```

Reported against the willingness-to-pay threshold, EVSI is a family of curves, one per sample size. Each stays between zero and the effect EVPPI. This trial measures only the health effect, so it does not resolve the cost uncertainty which limits the study's value below perfect information. A trial that also measured cost would rise toward EVPI instead. Larger trials shrink `tau`, lifting each curve toward the effect EVPPI.

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib import cm, colors

grid = np.linspace(0, 120_000, 13)
sizes = [25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800]
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
fan = {}
for n in sizes:  # the same study, evaluated across the threshold grid
    xbar = draws["dq"] + rng.normal(0.0, 1.0 / np.sqrt(n), len(draws))
    summ = pd.DataFrame({"xbar": xbar}, index=draws.index)
    fan[n] = [evsi_regression(outcomes, summ, k) for k in grid]

ramp, scale = plt.get_cmap("Purples"), colors.LogNorm(min(sizes), max(sizes))
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for n in sizes:
    ax.plot(grid, fan[n], color=ramp(0.3 + 0.6 * scale(n)), lw=1.4)
ax.plot(grid, evpi(outcomes, grid).to_numpy(), color="#333333", lw=2, label="EVPI")
ax.plot(grid, [evppi(outcomes, draws, "dq", k) for k in grid],
        color="#eb6834", lw=2, label="EVPPI (effect)")
ax.set(xlabel="willingness to pay", ylabel="value per person")
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
sm = cm.ScalarMappable(norm=scale, cmap=ramp); sm.set_array([])
fig.colorbar(sm, ax=ax, label="trial size (n per arm)");
```

## Expected net benefit of sampling (EBNS)

So, how many people should we sample? We can answer that question by calculating the expected net benefit of sampling (EBNS).

We calculate EBNS by multiplying the per-person EVSI by the beneficiary population and bring it to a present value estimate. For instance, suppose `2,000` patients a year would benefit from the study over `10` year. We discount those benefits at 3% per year, then subtract the study cost (say, a `300,000` fixed cost plus `6,000` per participant in each trial arm. An ENBS at or above zero is the condition for running the study. We can then investigate what is the trial size that maximizes the net expected benefit of sampling.

```{python}
i30 = int(np.where(grid == WTP)[0][0])
evsi = pd.Series({n: fan[n][i30] for n in sizes})  # EVSI at the reference threshold
years = np.arange(10)
beneficiaries = 2_000 * (1.03 ** -years).sum()  # discounted future patients
cost = 300_000 + 6_000 * 2 * np.array(sizes)  # 2n participants
enbs = pd.Series(beneficiaries * evsi.to_numpy() - cost, index=pd.Index(sizes, name="n per arm"))
best = enbs.idxmax()
print(f"optimal size: {best} per arm, ENBS {enbs[best]:,.0f}")
enbs.round(0)
```

```{python}
ax = enbs.plot(marker="o", xlabel="trial size (n per arm)", ylabel="ENBS")
ax.axhline(0, color="0.6", lw=0.8);
ax.plot(best, enbs[best], "r*", ms=14);
```
